request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"vine::social_communication","name":"vine","one_liner":"Twitter-owned short-form (6-second) looping video platform that grew to 200M users before being shut down in 2017 when creator exodus and advertiser flight made it unviable.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":1,"rationale":"Vine was a free, ad-supported consumer video platform with zero transaction fees and no paid 1:1 interaction model. The pitch is a B2C marketplace taking a 20% transaction fee on paid influencer-to-fan video consults — fundamentally different revenue architecture."},"market":{"score":3,"rationale":"Both serve consumers interested in short-form video and beauty/influencer content. Vine's beauty-adjacent meme culture and its role in launching influencers creates a surface overlap, but Vine targeted a global mass-market audience for passive entertainment, while the pitch targets a niche geographic market (Tokyo/Seoul) for premium, commerce-oriented 1:1 interactions."},"gtm":{"score":2,"rationale":"Vine relied on viral, organic growth and Twitter distribution for a global free product. The pitch requires recruiting specific supply-side beauty influencers in two cities and converting fans into paying customers — a high-touch, two-sided marketplace GTM with no meaningful parallel to Vine's approach."},"stage_scale":{"score":2,"rationale":"Vine at failure had 200M+ active users and was a Twitter subsidiary; it died at scale from strategic mismanagement and platform competition. The pitch is an early-stage startup. The scale, capital context, and failure mode are very different."}},"why_similar":"Both operate at the intersection of short-form video, influencers, and consumer social. Vine pioneered the creator/influencer economy and demonstrated that beauty and personality-driven content has strong consumer demand. Both depend on retaining key content creators (influencers) to sustain user engagement — creator retention was Vine's central unsolved problem and is directly relevant to the pitch.","where_diverged":"The pitch diverges on at least four concrete dimensions: (1) Monetization — the pitch charges a 20% transaction fee on paid 1:1 sessions vs. Vine's free ad-revenue model; (2) Geography — the pitch is explicitly scoped to Tokyo and Seoul (APAC niche) vs. Vine's global footprint; (3) Interaction model — the pitch enables synchronous paid video consults between influencer and fan rather than passive broadcast consumption; (4) Business model — the pitch is a two-sided B2C marketplace vs. Vine's single-sided social platform. These differences mean the pitch has a fundamentally different unit-economics profile and a much narrower, higher-intent audience.","failure_causes":["creator exodus to better-monetizing platforms (YouTube, Instagram)","no direct creator monetization mechanism on the platform","parent company (Twitter) strategic misalignment and cost-cutting","inability to compete with Instagram Video and Snapchat feature parity","advertiser flight as marketer budgets followed creators off-platform","six-second format constraint limiting creative depth and commercial use cases"],"lessons_for_input":["Build creator monetization into the core product from day one — never let influencers feel they earn more somewhere else","Design explicit supply-side lock-in (exclusive content, earnings dashboard, loyalty tiers) so influencers have a financial reason to stay","Validate that fans in Tokyo and Seoul will actually pay for 1:1 video access before scaling the supply side","Keep the platform's value proposition tightly differentiated from free alternatives like Instagram Live or TikTok LIVE so neither influencers nor fans can easily substitute","Avoid becoming dependent on a single parent company or distribution partner that could deprioritize or shut down the product for unrelated strategic reasons"]}
